section.title: Rehoboam and Shishak
section.sectionRationale: Once Rehoboam's rule is secure, he and all Judah forsake the Law of the LORD, and Shishak king of Egypt invades with a vast army, capturing the fortified cities and threatening Jerusalem itself. When the prophet Shemaiah announces that this is judgment for their unfaithfulness, Rehoboam and the leaders humble themselves, and the LORD relents, sparing Jerusalem from destruction though not from becoming Shishak's servants, so that Judah might learn the difference between serving Him and serving foreign kings.
section.thesis: Rehoboam's story shows that security breeds unfaithfulness, unfaithfulness invites judgment, but genuine humbling before the LORD, even midcrisis, turns aside destruction, though the consequences of forsaking God still leave their mark.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Rehoboam's unfaithfulness and Shishak's invasion, verses 1 to 4; Shemaiah's word and the humbling that turns aside destruction, verses 5 to 12; and the summary of Rehoboam's reign and death, verses 13 to 16.

units.label: Rehoboam's unfaithfulness and Shishak's invasion
units.note: he forsook the Law of the LORD

verses.text: After Rehoboam had established his sovereignty and royal power, he and all Israel with him forsook the {{torah|Law}} of the LORD.
In the fifth year of Rehoboam's reign, because they had been {{maal|unfaithful}} to the LORD, Shishak king of Egypt attacked Jerusalem
with 1,200 chariots, 60,000 horsemen, and countless troops who came with him out of Egypt, Libyans, Sukkites, and Cushites.
He captured the fortified cities of Judah and came as far as Jerusalem.

units.label: Shemaiah's word and the humbling that turns aside destruction

verses.text: Then Shemaiah the prophet came to Rehoboam and the leaders of Judah who had gathered at Jerusalem because of Shishak, and he said to them, "This is what the LORD says: 'You have forsaken Me; therefore, I have forsaken you into the hand of Shishak.'"
So the leaders of Israel and the king {{kana|humbled themselves}} and said, "The LORD is {{tsaddiq|righteous}}."

When the LORD saw that they had humbled themselves, the word of the LORD came to Shemaiah, saying, "They have humbled themselves; I will not destroy them, but will soon grant them deliverance. My wrath will not be poured out on Jerusalem through Shishak.
Nevertheless, they will become his servants, so that they may learn the difference between serving Me and serving the kings of other lands."
So King Shishak of Egypt attacked Jerusalem and seized the treasures of the house of the LORD and of the royal palace. He took everything, including the gold shields that Solomon had made.
Then King Rehoboam made bronze shields in their place and committed them to the care of the captains of the guard on duty at the entrance to the royal palace.
And whenever the king entered the house of the LORD, the guards would go with him, bearing the shields, and later they would return them to the guardroom.
Because Rehoboam humbled himself, the anger of the LORD turned away from him, and He did not destroy him completely. Indeed, conditions were good in Judah.

units.label: The summary of Rehoboam's reign and death
units.note: he did evil because he did not set his heart to seek the LORD

verses.text: Thus King Rehoboam established himself in Jerusalem and reigned. He was forty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem, the city the LORD had chosen from all the tribes of Israel in which to put His Name. His mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite.
And Rehoboam did evil because he did not set his heart to {{darash|seek}} the LORD.

Now the acts of Rehoboam, from first to last, are they not written in the records of Shemaiah the Prophet and of Iddo the Seer concerning the genealogies? There was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their days.
And Rehoboam rested with his fathers and was buried in the City of David. And his son Abijah reigned in his place.

torah.gloss: Law, instruction. Rehoboam and all Israel forsake the torah of the LORD once security makes them comfortable, showing that Israel's real danger was never external enemies first, but internal drift from the instruction that had once shaped David's and Solomon's obedience.

maal.gloss: Unfaithfulness, treachery, breach of trust. The Chronicler names Shishak's invasion as judgment because Judah had been maal, unfaithful, to the LORD, the same word used throughout Chronicles to describe covenant betrayal that invites disaster.

kana.gloss: To humble oneself, to be brought low. When Shemaiah announces judgment, the leaders and king kana, humble themselves, immediately, the very response God had promised to honor in Solomon's dedication prayer and in His own reply, 7:14, that healing follows genuine humbling.

tsaddiq.gloss: Righteous, just. Rehoboam and the leaders confess the LORD is tsaddiq, righteous, acknowledging that Shishak's invasion is not arbitrary cruelty but the just consequence of their own unfaithfulness, a confession that itself models the humility God honors.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, resort to. The chapter's verdict on Rehoboam's whole reign is that he did evil because he did not set his heart to darash, seek, the LORD, a single sentence that captures the root failure behind both his forsaking of the law and the crisis Shishak brought.

basic.context: Once Rehoboam feels secure on his throne, he and all Judah abandon the LORD's law, and God sends Shishak king of Egypt with an overwhelming army that captures Judah's fortified cities and reaches Jerusalem itself. The prophet Shemaiah explains plainly that this is judgment, you have forsaken Me, so I have forsaken you to Shishak. Remarkably, Rehoboam and the leaders immediately humble themselves and confess that the LORD is righteous, and God responds by sparing Jerusalem from full destruction, though Judah still becomes Shishak's tribute paying servant so that they might learn the difference between serving the LORD and serving foreign kings. The chapter closes with a stark summary: Rehoboam did evil because he never set his heart to seek the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific invasion and a specific royal response; it is not a promise that every crisis will be resolved by a single act of humbling, nor a claim that hardship always signals particular sin. What is timelessly true is the pattern the Chronicler consistently teaches: forsaking God invites real consequences, but genuine humility before Him, even during judgment, turns aside the worst of it, while shallow or absent seeking of the LORD defines a wasted reign.
basic.originalLanguages: Rehoboam and Israel forsake the torah, the LORD's law, once security sets in, an act of maal, unfaithfulness, that Shemaiah names as the direct cause of Shishak's invasion. Yet the moment the king and leaders kana, humble themselves, confessing the LORD is tsaddiq, righteous, judgment is tempered, while the chapter's final verdict is that Rehoboam never set his heart to darash, seek, the LORD at all.
basic.typology: Rehoboam's cycle of comfort, unfaithfulness, judgment, and partial repentance previews the whole pattern of Judah's kings that the rest of the book will trace, and stands in contrast to Christ, the perfectly faithful King who never forsakes His Father's law and whose humility before God is total and unbroken, Philippians 2:8. The shields of gold replaced by bronze picture a diminished glory, the shadow left behind whenever a people trade covenant faithfulness for compromise.
basic.christ: Where Rehoboam forsakes the law once comfort arrives and only humbles himself after judgment has already fallen, Christ perfectly kept His Father's law from first to last and humbled Himself not to escape judgment but to bear it fully on behalf of others, becoming obedient to the point of death, Philippians 2:8. The exchange of gold shields for bronze pictures the diminished glory that always follows unfaithfulness, a contrast to Christ, whose glory, once given up in humility, was fully restored and never diminished, Philippians 2:9-11.
basic.god: God disciplines His people justly when they forsake Him, yet He is quick to relent toward genuine humility, even humility that comes only after judgment has already begun to fall. He allows real, painful consequences, servitude to a foreign king, to teach a lesson comfort alone could not teach: that serving Him is altogether different from serving any other power.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 14:21-31 gives the parallel account of Rehoboam's reign and Shishak's invasion. James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5-6 both affirm that God gives grace to the humble. Philippians 2:6-11 traces Christ's humbling and subsequent exaltation, the perfect pattern Rehoboam's partial, self-interested humbling only faintly echoes. And 2 Chronicles 7:14 states the very principle this chapter narrates in action, humbling brings healing.
basic.oneStory: Rehoboam's comfortable unfaithfulness invites Shishak's invasion, yet his and Judah's swift humbling turns aside full destruction, even though real consequences remain, a pattern the rest of Judah's kings will repeat again and again. This cycle of forsaking, judgment, and humbling finds its resolution in Christ, the King who never forsook His Father, whose humbling was total and redemptive, and through whom lasting deliverance, not merely postponed judgment, is finally secured.

context.literary: The chapter is tightly compressed around a single theological principle, forsaking the LORD invites judgment while humbling before Him turns judgment aside, illustrated in three swift movements: sin and invasion, verses 1 to 4, prophetic word and humbling, verses 5 to 12, and a closing summary verdict on the whole reign, verses 13 to 16, that generalizes the specific incident into a final judgment on Rehoboam's character.
context.historical: Shishak, or Shoshenq I, founder of Egypt's Twenty-second Dynasty, is a well attested historical figure whose own military records at Karnak list numerous conquered towns in Israel and Judah, corroborating this biblical account of his campaign roughly five years after Solomon's death. The exchange of Solomon's gold shields for Rehoboam's bronze ones is a vivid, almost visual symbol the Chronicler uses to mark the visible diminishing of Judah's glory following unfaithfulness.
context.canonical: This chapter draws on and expands 1 Kings 14:21-31, adding the prophetic confrontation by Shemaiah and the account of humbling, verses 5 to 12, material absent from Kings, consistent with the Chronicler's larger pattern of showing that immediate repentance, even partial, brings real, if incomplete, relief from divine judgment, a lesson of urgent relevance to his exilic and post-exilic readers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate concretely the principle stated in 7:14, that humbling oneself before the LORD brings real, if not always complete, relief from deserved judgment, using Rehoboam's own reign as the first test case among the divided kingdom's many kings, so that the Chronicler's readers would recognize humility, however late, as always better than presumption or despair.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical invasion and a particular king's mixed record; it does not promise that every act of humbling will produce an identical outcome, sparing but not fully removing consequences. What is prescriptive and enduring is the call to humble oneself before God rather than remain in unfaithfulness, and the warning that comfort and security can breed exactly the kind of forgetfulness that invites discipline.

debates.question: Was Rehoboam's humbling in verse 6 genuine repentance or merely damage control?

views.label: Genuine, if incomplete, humility
views.case: The text records God Himself accepting the humbling as real, relenting from full destruction precisely because they had humbled themselves, verse 7; Scripture elsewhere shows God responding to even partial or crisis driven repentance, as with Ahab in 1 Kings 21:27-29, without requiring perfect motives before extending mercy.

views.label: A shallow, circumstantial response consistent with Rehoboam's overall pattern
views.case: The chapter's closing verdict, he did evil because he did not set his heart to seek the LORD, verse 14, suggests the humbling of verse 6 was situational rather than a lasting change of heart, explaining why judgment was mitigated but Judah still became Shishak's servant and Rehoboam's reign is summarized negatively overall.

debates.bearing: Both views can be held together: God graciously responds to real humbling even when it proves temporary or incomplete, sparing Jerusalem from destruction, while the lasting consequence of servitude and the negative verdict on Rehoboam's whole reign show that partial humility, however genuinely received by God in the moment, is no substitute for a heart that continually seeks Him.

study.originalLanguages: Once secure, Rehoboam and Israel forsake the torah, God's law, an act the text calls maal, unfaithfulness, and names as the direct trigger for Shishak's invasion. Yet the instant Shemaiah's word lands, king and leaders kana, humble themselves, confessing the LORD is tsaddiq, righteous, and judgment is tempered, even though the chapter's final verdict remains that Rehoboam never set his heart to darash, truly seek, the LORD across his whole reign.

typology.type: Rehoboam's cycle of comfort, unfaithfulness, judgment, and partial humbling
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with Christ's unbroken faithfulness and total, redemptive humbling, obedient even to death, Philippians 2:8.
The diminished glory that follows unfaithfulness, reversed in Christ, whose humbling led not to lesser glory but to the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: Where Rehoboam forsakes God's law once comfort sets in and only humbles himself after judgment has already begun to fall, Christ perfectly kept His Father's law without ever drifting, and His humbling was not self-preserving damage control but a willing descent to the point of death on a cross for the sake of others, Philippians 2:8. The visible diminishing of Judah's glory, gold shields exchanged for bronze, stands in stark contrast to Christ, whose humbling led not to lesser splendor but to exaltation and the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.
study.god: God disciplines unfaithfulness justly but is quick to relent toward genuine humility, even humility offered only in the midst of crisis. He permits real, lasting consequences, servitude to a foreign power, precisely to teach what comfort could not, that serving Him is nothing like serving any other king, and He remains attentive to the state of every heart, ultimately measuring a reign not by its outward stability but by whether it truly sought Him.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of Rehoboam's reign and Shishak's invasion.
States the principle this chapter narrates in action: humbling before the LORD brings healing and forgiveness.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, the pattern seen in Rehoboam's brief but real humbling.
Christ's total, redemptive humbling and subsequent exaltation, the perfect pattern Rehoboam's partial humbling only dimly echoes.

study.oneStory: Rehoboam's comfortable unfaithfulness invites Shishak's invasion, yet a swift, if incomplete, humbling turns aside full destruction, even as real consequences and a disappointing final verdict remain, a cycle the rest of Judah's kings will repeat again and again. This pattern of forsaking, judgment, and partial humbling is finally resolved in Christ, the King who never forsook His Father and whose complete, willing humbling secures not postponed judgment but everlasting deliverance for His people.

quiz.q: What led directly to Shishak's invasion of Judah, according to the text?

options.text: Once Rehoboam had established his power, he and all Israel forsook the Law of the LORD, an unfaithfulness that brought Shishak's attack in the fifth year of his reign.
options.note: Verses 1-2 state this cause and effect directly.

options.text: Egypt simply wanted to expand its empire regardless of Judah's spiritual condition.
options.note: The text explicitly ties the invasion to Judah's unfaithfulness, not merely Egyptian ambition.

options.text: Rehoboam had provoked Shishak through a military alliance with Israel's enemies.
options.note: No such alliance is mentioned; the stated cause is spiritual unfaithfulness, verse 2.

options.text: The chapter gives no reason for the invasion.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explicitly explain the invasion as judgment for unfaithfulness.

quiz.q: How did Rehoboam and the leaders of Judah respond to Shemaiah's prophetic word?

options.text: They humbled themselves and confessed, The LORD is righteous.
options.note: Verse 6 records this immediate, humble response.

options.text: They rejected Shemaiah's message and continued in defiance.
options.note: The opposite occurred; they responded with immediate humility, verse 6.

options.text: They fled the city in panic without any response to the prophet.
options.note: The text records a deliberate response of humility and confession, not panicked flight.

options.text: The text records no response at all.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly records their humbling and confession.

quiz.q: What was the outcome of this humbling, according to verses 7-8?

options.text: The LORD did not destroy them completely, but they still became Shishak's servants so they might learn the difference between serving the LORD and serving other kings.
options.note: Verses 7-8 record both the mercy shown and the remaining consequence.

options.text: All consequences were removed entirely and Judah paid no tribute at all.
options.note: The text explicitly says they would still become Shishak's servants, so some consequence remained.

options.text: Jerusalem was destroyed completely despite their humbling.
options.note: The text states the opposite; Jerusalem was spared from full destruction because of their humbling.

options.text: The text records no outcome from their humbling.
options.note: Verses 7-8 clearly describe both the mercy and remaining consequence.

quiz.q: What visible sign marked the diminished glory of Judah after Shishak's invasion?

options.text: Shishak took Solomon's gold shields, and Rehoboam replaced them with bronze shields instead.
options.note: Verses 9-10 describe this exact exchange.

options.text: The temple itself was completely destroyed and never rebuilt.
options.note: The temple's treasures were plundered, but the temple itself was not destroyed at this time; that destruction comes centuries later.

options.text: Rehoboam's crown was melted down and given to Shishak.
options.note: The text specifically describes the gold shields being taken and replaced with bronze, not the crown.

options.text: No treasures were taken at all.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly states Shishak seized the temple and palace treasures, including the gold shields.

quiz.q: What is the chapter's final verdict on Rehoboam's entire reign?

options.text: That he did evil because he did not set his heart to seek the LORD.
options.note: Verse 14 states this exact verdict as the summary of his reign.

options.text: That he was among the most faithful kings of Judah.
options.note: The text explicitly gives a negative verdict, not a commendation of faithfulness.

options.text: That his reign was marked by continuous peace with no conflict at all.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly notes there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their days.

options.text: The chapter offers no summary verdict on his reign.
options.note: Verse 14 gives an explicit, direct verdict on Rehoboam's character and reign.

questions: Rehoboam and Israel forsook the LORD's law once they felt secure. What comforts or successes in your own life tend to make you drift from seeking God?

The moment judgment was announced, Rehoboam and the leaders humbled themselves immediately. What keeps you from humbling yourself before God quickly rather than waiting until a crisis forces your hand?

God spared Jerusalem but still let Judah experience real consequences, so they would learn the difference between serving Him and serving other powers. What has a difficult season taught you about the difference between serving God and serving lesser things?

The chapter's final verdict on Rehoboam is that he never set his heart to truly seek the LORD, despite one moment of humbling. What would it look like for you to move from occasional crisis driven humility to a heart that continually seeks God?

Christ's humbling was total and led to His exaltation, unlike Rehoboam's partial, self-interested humbling. Where do you need to follow Christ's example of complete surrender rather than merely damage control?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote observes that Israel here refers to Judah, since in 2 Chronicles the Chronicler occasionally applies the name Israel to Judah as representative of the true covenant people even after the kingdom's division.
The Sukkites and Cushites named among Shishak's troops reflect the multi-ethnic composition of Egyptian armies of this period, with Cushites referring to people from the upper Nile region.
Abijah, who succeeds Rehoboam, is also called Abijam in the parallel account of 1 Kings 14:31, a variant form of the same name.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 12:1-16. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
